what_it_does: A React component that renders an interactive HTML5-canvas polar projection of a code/design ledger. It fetches projection data from /api/projection, optionally subscribes to live updates via /api/stream, and draws points under multiple analytic lenses: temporal (now/day/week cycles), semantic meaning-distance, two-pole separator gravity, nucleation type-birth, directed typed-edge connections, and hierarchical scale rungs. It supports picking points/sectors, scrubbing time, driving poles/centres, adjusting a birth dial, selecting a working set, and handing that set to a builder panel through a custom window event.
observations: The file is dominated by one large draw() callback that branches into mode-specific geometry: square registry box for nucleation, left/right basins for separator, chord diagram for connections, dyadic grid + concentric rings for the default wheel.
Inline comments are dense and refer to named design concepts: Tim Geldard's equation, SEED §111, Group 6/7/9/10/11, S4 frame-relativity, the 20/80 water-law.

It reads CSS custom properties (--tx, --line, --acc, --tx-3, --bg) at draw time so the canvas palette tracks the theme, while deliberately using hsl() angle-hue for points because colour is treated as geometry.
Live updates use EventSource /api/stream with a debounced refetch; the initial fetch establishes lastSeq so the stream only receives future events.
Animation is hand-rolled: position tweens use an easeOutCubic over 480 ms, scale rungs crossfade the outgoing point set, and NOW 'breathes' via requestAnimationFrame while live.
Nucleation re-computes the birth threshold client-side from the dial so the dial feels instant; the server still supplies size, distinct, and pile_clustered.
Error handling provides both a retry nonce and an escape back to the default lens when a bad binding/centre/pole/scrub is the cause.
The component dispatches a 'builder-context' CustomEvent to hand the selected working set to an external builder panel.
standouts: A single canvas renderer switches between six qualitatively different geometric readings of the same point set (time, meaning, polarity, type-birth, typed connections, scale).
The code is saturated with domain-language comments that map every visual choice to a named concept in the project's design vocabulary.
Client-side instant feedback for the nucleation birth dial without refetching the projection.
conventions: Follows React hooks conventions: useState/useRef/useEffect/useCallback for state, refs, side effects, and memoized draw.
Uses imperative canvas rendering rather than declarative SVG/React elements.
Reads design-system CSS variables at runtime for theming.
Breaks separation-of-concerns: geometry, rendering, interaction, and mode logic are all interleaved inside one large draw() function and nested helper closures.
Uses a global window CustomEvent as the seam to the builder panel.
concerns: The draw() function is very long and mode-heavy, making it hard to test, review, or extend without regressing another lens.
Many magic numbers and geometric constants are scattered inline (R_BOX_FRAC, SEP_SPREAD, rim fractions, animation durations, label thresholds).
Client-side birth logic duplicates server-side semantics; a mismatch would silently change which candidates appear born.
No canvas accessibility layer (aria labels, focus management, keyboard picking) is present in the file.
Performance relies on full-canvas redraw every frame with no visible culling or layer caching.
Optional fields on ProjPoint/Projection are numerous, so runtime assumptions (e.g., binding.poles, nucleation.zones) are weakly enforced.
notes: A single-file, multi-lens canvas renderer that turns a fetched projection into a live, manipulable polar map of a code/design ledger.
questions: What is the exact server contract for /api/projection and /api/stream, including all optional fields like phases, cell, strain, scale_children, and separation?
How are sector angles and kind mappings computed on the server, and do they guarantee the angular ordering the UI assumes?
What distinguishes source from address, and when is each safe to use as a semantic centre or pole?
How does the builder panel consume the 'builder-context' event, and what schema does it expect beyond the formatted block string?
What CSS rules are expected for classes like lattice-wrap, lattice-card, lc-sep, and lf-rungs?
Are the lenses mutually exclusive by construction, or should the UI guard against conflicting radius_from values?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the header frames it as a universal polar projection renderer, and the implementation indeed renders the same substrate through interchangeable temporal, semantic, separator, nucleation, connection, and scale lenses.
apparent_themes: Geometry as meaning: radius, angle, and colour encode analytic variables rather than decoration.
Live, continuous, event-driven data: SSE, breathing NOW, and smooth client-side animation.
Honest representation of uncertainty: r_unknown points sit at the rim, strain lines show structure↔meaning divergence, misfits pile outside the registry box.
Direct manipulation: pick a point to centre meaning, set a pole, zoom into a theme, or dial a birth threshold.
Identity-preserving transitions: points keep their seq and slide to new positions instead of teleporting.
intention_signals: The file is trying to make abstract registry, embedding, and temporal relationships spatially explorable through a consistent polar interaction language, where switching an analytic lens changes the geometry but keeps the same picking, animation, and selection mechanics.
novelty: The unusual aspect is the co-existence of so many distinct analytic geometries (separator basins, nucleation square, connections chord diagram, scale crossfade) inside one canvas component, documented with a project-specific philosophical vocabulary.
contribution: A reusable canvas projection renderer with DPR scaling, CSS-variable theming, and a hand-rolled animation/crossfade framework; a multi-lens interaction model that maps time, meaning, polarity, type-birth, typed connections, and hierarchical scale to the same polar canvas; a live SSE update pattern with debounced coalescing; and a working-set handoff mechanism to a builder panel.
summary_for_embedding: LatticeView.tsx is a React canvas component that fetches a multi-lens polar projection from /api/projection, renders it with mode-specific geometry for time, meaning, polarity, type-birth, typed connections, and scale, supports live SSE updates, point/sector picking, and hands a selected working set to a builder via a custom window event.
intention_for_embedding: It was built to let operators explore a code/design ledger through interchangeable geometric lenses that make time, meaning, polarity, type birth, typed connections, and hierarchical scale visible in one interactive canvas.

symbols.does: Defines the shape of a single projected point in the lattice.
symbols.description: TypeScript type carrying angular, radial, structural, temporal, semantic, separator, scale, and nucleation fields for one item; includes optional fields for source key, unknown radius, pole lean, structural strain, and theme aggregation.

symbols.does: Defines the shape of the whole projection response.
symbols.description: TypeScript type describing projection metadata (now, n sectors, rings, count, grid), the active binding, available bindings, sectors, points, plus optional separator statistics, directed edges, scale ladder, and nucleation report.

symbols.does: The main React component that renders the interactive lattice canvas and controls.
symbols.description: Exports the LatticeView component; manages state for projection data, picking, working set, zoom, frame, rung, live/scrubbed time, centre, poles, nucleation axes, and coordinates canvas drawing, SSE subscription, animation, and user actions.

symbols.does: Formats a scrubbed epoch as a human-readable relative time string.
symbols.description: Computes seconds between the live anchor and the given epoch, then returns phrases like '3h 12m ago' or 'moments ago' for the foot readout.

symbols.does: Maps a point's separator pole to a left/right basin angle.
symbols.description: Geometry helper used by both draw() and pick(); places pole A on the left axis, pole B on the right axis, and neutral at the top, fanning within each basin by the original kind-angle.

symbols.does: Shortens a pole identifier for display.
symbols.description: Returns a friendly binding label unchanged, or the last two path segments of an address-like pole so the UI never shows a full vec:// identifier or stale default.

symbols.does: Computes the radial fraction for a nucleation point relative to the registry box.
symbols.description: Returns a fraction of the canvas radius: points that fit a registered type sit inside the inscribed square (scaled by R_BOX_FRAC), while piled points are pushed outside the box into a rim band.

symbols.does: Extracts a concise trailing segment from a path/address string.
symbols.description: Strips leading/trailing slashes, takes the last segment, and removes common file extensions; used for sector labels, pole tags, theme exemplars, and nucleation candidates.

symbols.does: Steps the scale ladder one level finer.
symbols.description: Looks up the current rung in the coarse-to-fine ladder and returns the next entry, or null when already at units; supports the 'zoom into theme' gesture.

symbols.does: Resolves the normalized radial coordinate for a point under the active lens/frame.
symbols.description: Returns the radius used for drawing: meaning-distance/lean/fit for semantic/separator/nucleation, age-from-now for 'now', or the day/week cycle coordinate for cyclic frames, with zoom applied where appropriate.

symbols.does: Responds to canvas pointer down by selecting a sector or point.
symbols.description: Maps the click to canvas coordinates; in connections mode it selects the sector under the angle (or clears near centre), otherwise it finds the nearest point using the same geometry as draw() and updates picked.

symbols.does: Maps a day-phase fraction to a human-readable time-of-day word.

symbols.does: Dispatches the current selection to the builder panel.
symbols.description: Formats the selected points into a markdown-ish block and fires a 'builder-context' custom event on window, then calls the optional onHandoff callback to reveal the builder.

symbols.does: Returns a readable sector label by index.
symbols.description: Helper used in the connections readout; gives the sector label or a fallback index for the picked row's outgoing and incoming edges.

symbols.code_id: code://company/canvas/app/src/regions/LatticeView.tsx::nuBorn | function | const nuBorn = (size: number, distinct: boolean) => !!distinct && size >= nuBirthMass
symbols.does: Client-side predicate for whether a nucleation candidate is born.
